items.headline: Circio completes NOK300m financing to fund circular RNA platform development
items.preview: Circio has secured NOK300m to advance its circVec circular RNA expression platform.
items.article:
Oslo-based Circio has completed a NOK300m (€27m) financing round, extending its runway to the end of 2030 and providing funds to advance its circVec circular RNA expression platform toward IND-enabling development and clinical proof-of-concept.
The financing was completed through the exercise of warrants and an underwritten private placement, bringing Circio's total capital raised in the first half of 2026 to approximately NOK620m (€57m). This funding is aimed at supporting preclinical development of circVec and moving a lead gene therapy program toward an investigational new drug filing.
Why this matters: This funding is transformational for Circio, allowing acceleration of preclinical development and positioning the company as a significant player in gene and cell therapies with its innovative circular RNA technology.

items.headline: Boehringer/Zealand obesity drug delivers Phase III weight loss, but side effects raise questions
items.preview: Boehringer's survodutide shows significant weight loss in Phase III trials but raises tolerability concerns.
items.article:
Boehringer Ingelheim has reported new Phase III data for survodutide, a dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist for obesity, showing up to 16.6% body weight loss after 76 weeks. The drug also reduced visceral fat and liver fat significantly compared to placebo.
However, 19% of patients discontinued treatment due to gastrointestinal side effects, raising questions about its tolerability. The drug's differentiation may lie in its liver effects rather than just weight loss, as it activates both GLP-1 and glucagon receptors.
Why this matters: The Phase III results support survodutide's potential in metabolic health, but its gastrointestinal side effects could impact its market competitiveness against established therapies.

items.headline: How Chaperones Build RNA Silencing Complexes
items.preview: New insights reveal how molecular chaperones assist in the assembly of RNA silencing complexes.
items.article:
Scientists have decrypted the structural dynamics of the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC) assembly, highlighting the role of molecular chaperones in the maturation of Argonaute (AGO) proteins. This discovery reveals that AGO loading with RNA is an actively chaperoned process rather than a passive binding event.
Using cryogenic electron microscopy, researchers captured the AGO maturation complex, showing how AGO undergoes significant conformational changes to accept RNA duplexes. This study provides insights into the RNA loading mechanism and the importance of specific RNA features for AGO maturation.
Why this matters: Understanding the assembly of RISC and the role of chaperones could lead to advancements in RNA-based therapeutics, enhancing their efficacy and stability.

items.headline: Smoothened and ciliary GPCRs regulate ciliary protein kinase A activity involved in Hedgehog signal transduction
items.preview: Research uncovers the role of Smoothened and GPCRs in regulating PKA activity in Hedgehog signaling.
items.article:
This study investigates how Hedgehog (HH) signaling in vertebrates relies on the primary cilium and the regulation of protein kinase A (PKA) activity by Smoothened (SMO) and ciliary GPCRs. The findings suggest that SMO inhibits PKA to activate HH signaling, while GPR161 enhances PKA activity.
The research provides insights into the molecular mechanisms of HH signal transduction and highlights the importance of ciliary localization of PKA for its activity. This work advances our understanding of the intricate signaling pathways involved in development and cellular communication.
Why this matters: Understanding the regulation of PKA in HH signaling could have implications for developmental biology and the treatment of related disorders.

items.headline: Estrogen impacts NOD2-dependent regulation of intestinal homeostasis
items.preview: Study reveals a link between estrogen signaling and NOD2's role in intestinal health.
items.article:
This research explores how NOD2, a key receptor in Crohn's disease, regulates intestinal homeostasis and its interaction with estrogen signaling. Using a zebrafish model, the study shows that NOD2 deficiency leads to significant intestinal defects and increased expression of estrogen-response genes.
Functional assays indicate that estrogen exposure replicates NOD2-deficiency effects, while tamoxifen treatment can reverse these defects, suggesting a regulatory axis between NOD2 and estrogen that is crucial for gut health.
Why this matters: These findings provide insights into the mechanisms underlying Crohn's disease and highlight the potential role of hormonal signaling in disease susceptibility.
